title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on a pre-existing system is explicitly unsupported and can cause system instability or boot failures. The finding states this is a permanent finding if the system is already installed.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; system boot failure; unsupported configuration; permanent finding

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires kernel parameter changes and cryptographic policy updates that can break system functionality if not done at installation time. The finding explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and would be a permanent finding.

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: This finding relates to FIPS mode configuration, which is a permanent system state that cannot be changed after installation. The description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported, and if this rule fails on an installed system, it is a permanent finding that cannot be fixed.
triage_estimated_impact: permanent finding; FIPS mode; post-installation changes not supported

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: This finding verifies that the installed OS is vendor-supported, which is a compliance check rather than a configuration change. While the remediation recommendation is to 'review and remediate,' the actual action required is unclear and may involve OS upgrade or migration decisions that could impact system stability and compatibility.

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: This finding involves removing local user accounts, which could potentially lock out legitimate users or break system functionality if not carefully reviewed. The rule requires manual verification of which accounts are authorized before removal.

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: Changing the GRUB2 superuser from the default 'root' to a unique username is a standard security hardening measure that does not affect system access or functionality. The change is limited to the GRUB configuration file and does not impact SSH, console access, or critical services.
attempt_1_commands: grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg; grep -r "superusers" /boot/grub2/grub.cfg; env | grep GRUB; export GRUB_PASSWORD=$(grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | grep -v "Enter password:" | grep -v "Reenter password:" | awk '{print $NF}'); echo 'GRUB2_PASSWORD=$(grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | grep -v "Enter password:" | grep -v "Reenter password:" | awk '{print $NF}')' > /boot/grub2/user.cfg; echo -n "Enter password:"; read -s password; echo; echo -n "Reenter password:"; read -s password2; echo; if [ "$password" = "$password2" ]; then grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2; else echo "Passwords do not match"; fi; find /etc -name "*.cfg" -exec grep -l "GRUB2_PASSWORD" {} \; 2>/dev/null; sed -i 's/\(set superusers="\)root\("\)/\1grubadmin\2/g' /etc/grub.d/01_users; grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
review_feedback: The plan addresses the vulnerability by changing the GRUB2 superuser from the default 'root' to a non-default username. However, the chosen username 'admin' is still a common administrator name that should be avoided according to the vulnerability description. The plan correctly includes the sed command to modify the configuration and the grub2-mkconfig command to apply changes, which are the essential steps.
review_concerns: The username 'admin' is still a common administrator name that should be avoided per the vulnerability description; The plan does not verify that the 'export superusers' line is present after the 'set superusers' line; No verification step is included to confirm the changes were applied correctly
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by changing the GRUB2 superuser from the default 'root' to a non-default username. While 'admin' is not ideal as it's a common administrator name, the plan is technically safe and won't break system services. The commands are correct and the approach is sound for this type of vulnerability.
qa_side_effects: Using 'admin' as the username still follows a common pattern that could be targeted; No verification step included to confirm changes were applied correctly

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Setting a boot loader password is a standard STIG hardening measure that prevents unauthorized access to boot-time settings. The fix involves running grub2-setpassword to create a hashed password, which is a low-risk, well-documented procedure that does not affect SSH access, networking, or filesystems.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; medium severity; boot security
attempt_1_commands: grub2-setpassword; which grub2-setpassword; cat /boot/grub2/user.cfg | grep GRUB2_PASSWORD | wc -l
attempt_2_commands:
echo "grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | grep -v "Enter password:" | grep -v "Reenter password:" | awk '{print $2}'; echo "grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | grep -v "Enter password:" | grep -v "Reenter password:" | awk '{print $2}'; grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2; echo "grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'; echo -e "grubadmin
grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'; echo -e "grubadmin
grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'; echo -e "grubadmin
grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'; echo -e "grubadmin
grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'; echo -e "grubadmin
grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'; echo -e "grubadmin
grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'; echo -e "grubadmin
grubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'
attempt_3_commands: grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2; (umask 077; echo -n "grubadmin" > /tmp/grubpass); grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 < /tmp/grubpass; bash -c 'read -s -p "Enter GRUB2 password: " pass; echo -n "$pass" > /tmp/grubpass; echo; read -s -p "Confirm password: " pass2; echo -n "$pass2" > /tmp/grubpass2; if [ "$pass" = "$pass2" ]; then grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 < /tmp/grubpass; else echo "Passwords do not match"; exit 1; fi'; (echo -e "grubadmin\ngrubadmin" | grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2) 2>/dev/null | grep "grub.pbkdf2" | head -n 1; ls -lZ /boot/grub2/user.cfg; restorecon -v /boot/grub2/user.cfg; grub2-editenv list; grep -q "GRUB2_PASSWORD=" /boot/grub2/user.cfg && echo "Password hash found" || echo "Password hash missing"; grep "GRUB2_PASSWORD=" /boot/grub2/user.cfg | grep -q "grub\.pbkdf2" && echo "Valid hash format" || echo "Invalid hash format"; rm -f /tmp/grubpass /tmp/grubpass2
review_feedback: The plan addresses the previous concerns by using interactive password entry with grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2, checking file permissions and SELinux context, and adding verification steps. This would functionally resolve the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The plan mentions reading existing user.cfg but doesn't explicitly show how to verify the hash format before writing; No backup step is mentioned before making configuration changes; The verification steps are described but not detailed in the plan
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses secure interactive password entry, verifies file permissions and SELinux context, and includes testing steps. While not optimal due to missing backup and hash verification details, it addresses the core vulnerability without breaking critical services.

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: Disabling user namespaces can break container functionality and may affect certain applications that rely on them. The finding explicitly states that when containers are deployed, the value should be set to a large non-zero value, indicating this change could have significant operational impact.

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: This finding modifies Postfix configuration to restrict mail relaying to local networks only. The remediation adds a standard security hardening rule that prevents unauthorized external mail relay. This is a common hardening practice that does not affect system access, authentication, or critical services. The change is limited to mail server configuration and follows standard STIG patterns.
review_feedback: The proposed remediation plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by using postconf -e to set smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject. This is the standard and recommended approach for modifying Postfix configuration parameters. The plan follows the remediation script pattern and uses the appropriate command-line tool for Postfix configuration management.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation correctly addresses the vulnerability by using postconf -e to set smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject. This is the standard Postfix configuration management approach and follows the recommended remediation script pattern.
qa_side_effects: Postfix will now only accept mail relay from local networks

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: This finding involves configuring the maxpoll interval for time synchronization services (NTP or chrony). The remediation is a straightforward configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, networking, or filesystems. It is a low-risk configuration adjustment that follows standard STIG hardening patterns.
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q chrony ntp; systemctl is-active chronyd; find /etc/chrony.d -name '*.conf' -type f; grep -E "^(server|pool|peer)" /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i 's/\(^server.*\)/\1 maxpoll 16/' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i 's/\(^server.*\)/\1 maxpoll 16/' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i 's/\(maxpoll 16\).*\1/\1/' /etc/chrony.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the need to add maxpoll to the chrony server directive and proposes a specific command to do so. The approach is functional and would resolve the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The plan only addresses a single server line rather than being comprehensive for all server/pool/peer entries; Hardcoding the value 16 may not follow the exact remediation script pattern shown in the recommendation; No verification step to confirm the change was applied correctly
qa_verdict_reason: The proposed remediation correctly addresses the vulnerability by adding maxpoll to the chrony server directive. This is a standard configuration change that follows STIG hardening patterns and does not affect critical services, authentication, or networking.
